title: Freeform weaving on a frame loom
subtitle: Weaving without a fixed pattern: combining textures, colours, and materials freely
excerpt: Freeform weaving uses a frame loom as a surface for textile composition rather than the production of a technically specified cloth. There are no rules about which threads to use, how to alternate them, or how to beat. The weaver responds to what is being made, changing colour and texture as the piece grows. Freeform weaving is a distinct practice with its own conventions: it is not weaving without care but weaving without a predetermined structure.
sourceNotes: Freeform frame-loom weaving developed as a textile art practice in the mid-twentieth century. Technique is observation-led.

content.text: Freeform weaving on a frame loom begins with a warped surface and an open set of materials: different weights and textures of yarn, strips of fabric, natural fibres, even twigs and feathers woven in as structural elements. The
changes from row to row as the composition develops.
add pile and fringe at any point in the piece.

items.name: Warp thread, smooth and strong (cotton or linen at 8 EPI)
Weft yarns in varied weights and colours

content.text: Warp the frame at 8 EPI in a natural or neutral colour (so the warp is not visually dominant). A plain tabby warp serves all techniques that will be applied above it.
Step 2: Establish a plain weave base
Weave 3 to 5 rows of plain tabby weave at the bottom to create a stable anchor for subsequent rows. This base can be in a neutral colour or the primary accent colour of the piece.

Work in horizontal bands, changing weft material or colour at any point. Weaving does not need to proceed straight across the full width: a section of colour can be woven into one area while the adjacent area stays open. The visual logic of the piece drives the decisions rather than a technical pattern. Vary the beating: dense rows lie flat and dense; loose rows are open and textural.
Step 4: Add rya knots for texture

To add a rya knot: fold a length of yarn in half over two warp threads. Bring both ends forward between the two threads. Pull down firmly. The two ends hang as a fringe pair. Work a row of rya knots at any point in the weaving and follow with two plain weave rows to lock them in place.

attrs.intro: Two problems that arise in freeform weaving.

items.fix: Arc the weft loosely before beating each row. Maintain consistent selvedge width by checking against the original warp width every 10 rows.
items.cause: Weft packed too tightly, pulling the warp inward.
items.symptom: Warp pulling inward at the selvedges over several rows

items.fix: When changing yarn: weave the tail back into the same shed for 2 to 3 cm using a darning needle before cutting. The tail locks under the weft threads.
items.cause: Ends were not woven back into the work when changing colours.
items.symptom: Weft tails showing at the surface

attrs.heading: Common problems
